title: Matchmaker
content:
Soundtracks are symphonies connected to images.

I enjoy playing them in the store and listening to the reactions. Sometimes it's name that tune. Sometimes it's a direct reaction to a favorite show or movie.

And immediately we're in a conversation about their last movie and how we missed Maggie Smith.

Occasionally it's a bridge.

And the book finds a new home.

We love a good connection.

We connect the dots and the world doesn't seem random.

Music connects the dots. As do books.

I play the music and sell the books.

And I am a matchmaker.

title: The Precedent
content:
A man brought several games to our swap this weekend.

Tickets in hand, he glanced at the rows of books around the shop.

He smiled.

I wonder where they become just things. Where does simplifying or downsizing become strong enough for us to let go?

Maybe that's why the swaps work — if we let go, someone else takes these things on.

Or sometimes we don't want to set the precedent.

title: I'll Remember That
content:
It's something I say to myself all the time.

A book someone recommended. An idea I had early in the morning. Something I mean to do later.

Often, I do remember. Enough to keep telling myself I'll remember it.

But sometimes, it's just quietly gone.

I tell myself if it was a good idea, I'll remember it.

Really?

Memory isn't built to hold onto everything that matters.

But I have no way of remembering if that's true.

title: Thinking About Someone
content:
There are people I think about from time to time.

I'll be walking, or driving, or doing something ordinary, and they come to mind.

And I think, I should reach out.

But I don't.

It isn't because I don't care. The moment passes. The day fills in around it.

Thinking about someone is not the same as showing up for them.

It feels close enough that your brain lets you believe you've done something.

But you haven't.

title: The Thing You Keep Moving
content:
There's usually at least one. The thing that survives every cleanup.

You pick it up, move it somewhere else, and keep going. Or more often, you just ignore it.

It's not something you need right now. But you don't get rid of it.

It just… waits.

I've noticed this recently — how some things never quite get resolved.

They exist in some kind of middle space. Not valuable enough to use but not unnecessary enough to remove.

I have these in my life.

Things that continue simply because they haven't made any noise.

Years ago I purchased the domain terryheath.com. I did some things with it here and there. And paid to renew it year after year.

I didn't want to let it go, and it wasn't making any noise.

In The Holiday, a retired Hollywood writer tells Kate Winslet's character she's a leading lady who's been acting like the best friend.

Seems like it's time to dust off terryheath.com. You're supposed to be the leading character of your own life, for God's sake!

So here I am.

With the little things in our lives: the stories, the people, the things. Thoughts about dealing with them intentionally.

I've often said everyone has a right to my opinion. I hope you'll stick around awhile and exercise your rights.

title: About
content:
I own a bookstore in a small Washington State town and taught college English for 14 years.

I also build apps, do art and draw comics, make card games, and write a weekly letter, Small Things. That's where you've landed.

All this is more connected than it seems.

The bookstore is Sinclair Inlet Book Co. People come and go and say things they might not realize I've heard. Sometimes that's how this letter starts.

They talk about the people and things in their lives. The apps are about these things. Wishlists, small tools for not letting things disappear.

The card games and comics try to make something enjoyable out of the chaos these things bring.

I've often said everyone has a right to my opinion.

This is where I exercise that. This is where I show up.

If you want the weekly letter, tap the button to subscribe.

— Terry
